replies.text:
@robb233  As someone who has actually read economics, let me address this. Capitalist Theorists of the 1800s ran into the same issue that Marxism is entirely based upon when it comes to international trade and free markets. Good in theory, bad in practice, worse when you think about it. Their idea was to pass on the savings generated from international trade to the people who would benefit from companies being able to make the product cheaper. Yes, they quite literally wrote that they expected business to be altruistic. They weren't fools; they expected competition would force their hands, so the lack of passing on the savings to the masses would only be temporary. Yes, they quite literally assumed business would not collude to rig prices. Then, in the early 1900s, they did exactly that. Both privately, which is now illegal, and publicly, with Anti-Competition Laws. 

Let's move beyond the fact that this doesn't pass the fridge logic examination to other inherent truths. We do not have a Free Market system. We have a heavily regulated Corporatist system. When the writers that hatched these ideas wrote their works, Corporations as we know them today, quite literally, didn't exist. Corporations are classic socialist inventions. They redistribute wealth, shield operators from the consequences of their actions (an anti free market idea), and are given special legal status by the government. Free Market thinkers would have opposed their existence as they are antithetical to the free market. Even then, if the markets did not have a mountain of protectionist or barrier to entry laws.

We live in a system where we have a Neo Feudalism. An elite class that moves between corporations like Feudal Lords. A managerial class that goes to the correct schools and then into positions of power. Then we have the serfs, the working class whom occassionally can rise into the managerial class. None of this is anything the writers on the free market envisionsed in their writings.

Now for the worst part. The same writers also warned and were opposed to the ideas of classic socialism. Governments back then weren't all classic socialist utopias we have today. They didn't own the roads, they didn't control the schools, they didn't control medicine, they were bloated, uncontrollable behemoths that have reached such size they can no longer sustain themselves biologically. To have no tariffs would mean giving up government control over education, medicine, roads, and other areas.  You cannot leave out that important detail. Tariffs are needed for state spending, or they have to excessively tax businesses and steal from the population in the form of income taxation.

Yes, tariffs caused industries to be lazy. There is no perfect system. Free Market writers weren't pigheaded. If they had seen what we have today, they would have rewritten much of what they wrote. Or would have said if you do it the way they envisioned, tariffs wouldn't be neccessary. They didn't envision business moving out of country or the economic impact the loss of business has on local economies. They were ideolistic in only caring about the benifits for the people without counterweighing the costs. Others were for tariffs for these exact reasons. Capitalism isn't a unified school. You have Adam Smith's work, often called Free Market Capitalism; you have the work of Carl Menger and the school it spawned, the Austrian School of Capitalism; and then you have the work of Keynesianism. Lastly you have the work of socialists as they too utilize capital and their systems are thus forms of capitalism.

Free Market idea of no tariffs only works in a free market system. Applying it beyond is an act that will only result in more suffering. American economic history has shown this to be true.
